questions.question: In GPU compute, what is the relationship between a grid, a workgroup, and a thread?
options: A grid contains workgroups, each workgroup contains threads that share memory and can synchronize
A workgroup contains grids, and threads run independently across all grids
Threads are grouped into grids, and multiple grids form a workgroup
Grids, workgroups, and threads are three independent dispatch dimensions with no hierarchy
questions.explanation: The GPU thread hierarchy is grid → workgroup → thread. A dispatch creates a grid of workgroups; each workgroup contains threads that share fast on-chip memory and can coordinate via barriers. Threads in different workgroups cannot communicate during a single dispatch.

questions.question: Why is shared/workgroup memory (CUDA __shared__, WGSL var<workgroup>) faster than global/device memory?
options: It uses a different addressing scheme that the compiler optimizes better
It resides on-chip close to the ALUs, while global memory is off-chip with ~100x higher latency
It is compressed by the GPU hardware before storage
It is automatically cached by the L1 cache, whereas global memory bypasses all caches
questions.explanation: Shared/workgroup memory is physically located on the GPU chip close to the streaming multiprocessor (SM), giving ~5 cycle access vs ~400-800 cycles for global (off-chip) memory. This is not caching — it's a separate, programmer-managed memory space. It is not compressed, and it is distinct from the L1 cache.

questions.question: What happens if you remove the barrier (CUDA __syncthreads / WGSL workgroupBarrier) from a reduction kernel that uses shared memory?
options: The kernel will crash with a runtime error
The GPU will automatically reorder threads to maintain correctness
Some threads may read stale values from shared memory because writes from other threads are not yet visible
Performance will improve but results will be identical because GPU threads execute in lockstep
questions.explanation: Without a barrier, there is no guarantee that one thread's write to shared memory is visible to other threads before they read it. This is a data race. The GPU does NOT execute all threads in lockstep (warps are lockstep within a warp, but not across warps). The kernel won't crash — it will silently produce wrong results.

questions.question: A tree reduction of n elements has O(log n) depth. How many total operations does it perform?
options: O(log n) — each step does one operation
O(n log n) — each of log n steps processes all n elements
O(n²) — every thread compares against every other thread
questions.explanation: The tree reduction performs n/2 additions in the first step, n/4 in the second, and so on down to 1. This geometric series sums to n-1 total operations, which is O(n). The key advantage over sequential is not fewer total operations, but that the operations are parallelized across O(log n) sequential steps (depth).

questions.question: Which pair correctly maps CUDA compute shader concepts to their WebGPU/WGSL equivalents?
options: blockIdx → @global_id, threadIdx → @local_invocation_id, __shared__ → var<workgroup>
blockIdx → @local_invocation_id, threadIdx → @global_id, __shared__ → var<storage>
blockIdx → @workgroup_id, threadIdx → @local_invocation_id, __shared__ → var<workgroup>
gridDim → @workgroup_size, blockIdx → @local_invocation_id, __constant__ → var<workgroup>
questions.explanation: blockIdx identifies which workgroup (block) you're in — that's @workgroup_id. threadIdx identifies which thread within the block — that's @local_invocation_id. __shared__ memory is shared within a block/workgroup — that's var<workgroup>. @global_id combines both (like blockIdx*blockDim+threadIdx). var<storage> maps to global/device memory, not shared.

questions.question: When does using a GPU compute shader typically NOT outperform a CPU implementation?
options: When processing arrays with millions of elements that are already on the GPU
When running a parallel reduction on data that must first be transferred from CPU memory to GPU memory and the result transferred back, for a small array
When performing a tree reduction where each step does the same operation on half of the previous step's data
When using shared memory to reduce global memory traffic within a workgroup
questions.explanation: GPU compute shines when data is already on the GPU or when the parallel workload is large enough to amortize transfer latency. For small datasets that require CPU→GPU→CPU transfer, the PCIe transfer overhead dominates and the CPU wins. The other scenarios (large data, tree reduction, shared memory optimization) are all cases where GPUs excel.
